title: The Umbrella Repair Shop
text_zh: 在老城区的一条小巷子里，有一家专门修伞的店。店主是一个六十多岁的老师傅，每天早上八点准时开门。他把收到的坏伞一把一把地摆在门口，然后一边听收音机，一边慢慢地修。有人问他：「现在买一把新伞比修伞还便宜，为什么还要开这家店？」他笑着说：「因为有些伞是别人送的，扔了可惜。我帮他们把记忆修好。」除了修伞以外，他有时候还帮邻居修椅子、修钟。整条巷子的人都叫他「万能师傅」。虽然赚的钱不多，但是他看起来比谁都满足。
text_pinyin: Zài lǎo chéngqū de yì tiáo xiǎo xiàngzi lǐ, yǒu yì jiā zhuānmén xiū sǎn de diàn. Diànzhǔ shì yí gè liùshí duō suì de lǎo shīfu, měi tiān zǎoshang bā diǎn zhǔnshí kāi mén. Tā bǎ shōu dào de huài sǎn yì bǎ yì bǎ de bǎi zài ménkǒu, ránhòu yìbiān tīng shōuyīnjī, yìbiān mànman de xiū. Yǒu rén wèn tā: 「Xiànzài mǎi yì bǎ xīn sǎn bǐ xiū sǎn hái piányi, wèishénme hái yào kāi zhè jiā diàn?」 Tā xiàozhe shuō: 「Yīnwèi yǒuxiē sǎn shì biéren sòng de, rēng le kěxī. Wǒ bāng tāmen bǎ jìyì xiū hǎo.」 Chúle xiū sǎn yǐwài, tā yǒu shíhou hái bāng línjū xiū yǐzi, xiū zhōng. Zhěng tiáo xiàngzi de rén dōu jiào tā「wànnéng shīfu」. Suīrán zhuàn de qián bù duō, dànshì tā kàn qǐlái bǐ shéi dōu mǎnzú.
text_en: In a small alley in the old district, there's a shop that specializes in repairing umbrellas. The owner is a craftsman in his sixties who opens at eight sharp every morning. He lines up the broken umbrellas at the doorway one by one, then slowly repairs them while listening to the radio. Someone asked him: 'A new umbrella is cheaper than repairing one now — why keep this shop?' He smiled and said: 'Because some umbrellas were gifts. Throwing them away would be a waste. I help people repair their memories.' Besides umbrellas, he sometimes fixes chairs and clocks for neighbors. Everyone in the alley calls him the 'do-it-all master.' Although he doesn't earn much, he looks more content than anyone.

title: The Midnight Convenience Store
text_zh: 凌晨两点的便利店，灯亮着，像城市最后一盏没有关的灯。进来的人都不怎么说话。有穿西装的人买啤酒，有刚下班的护士买面包。收银员是个年轻的女生，她从来不问「还需要别的吗」——她知道这个时间来的人，只想安安静静地买完就走。我每次加班到很晚，都会来这里买一杯热牛奶。虽然我跟收银员从来没有聊过天，但是她每次都会把牛奶放进一个纸袋子里，比较不容易冷。这种不说话的温柔，比很多话都让人感动。
text_pinyin: Língchén liǎng diǎn de biànlì diàn, dēng liàngzhe, xiàng chéngshì zuìhòu yì zhǎn méiyǒu guān de dēng. Jìnlái de rén dōu bù zěnme shuō huà. Yǒu chuān xīzhuāng de rén mǎi píjiǔ, yǒu gāng xiàbān de hùshi mǎi miànbāo. Shōuyínyuán shì ge niánqīng de nǚshēng, tā cónglái bú wèn「hái xūyào biéde ma」——tā zhīdào zhège shíjiān lái de rén, zhǐ xiǎng ān'ānjìngjìng de mǎi wán jiù zǒu. Wǒ měi cì jiābān dào hěn wǎn, dōu huì lái zhèlǐ mǎi yì bēi rè niúnǎi. Suīrán wǒ gēn shōuyínyuán cónglái méiyǒu liáo guò tiān, dànshì tā měi cì dōu huì bǎ niúnǎi fàng jìn yí gè zhǐ dàizi lǐ, bǐjiào bù róngyì lěng. Zhè zhǒng bù shuō huà de wēnróu, bǐ hěn duō huà dōu ràng rén gǎndòng.
text_en: The convenience store at two in the morning, lights on, like the city's last lamp still burning. People who come in barely speak. Someone in a suit buys beer; a nurse just off shift buys bread. The cashier is a young woman who never asks 'need anything else?' — she knows people who come at this hour just want to buy quietly and leave. Every time I work overtime late, I come here for hot milk. Though I've never chatted with the cashier, she always puts my milk in a paper bag so it stays warm longer. This wordless gentleness is more moving than many words.

title: The Bench Between Buildings
text_zh: 我们小区的两栋楼之间，有一张被人忘了的长椅。春天的时候，阳光刚好照到那里，暖暖的。我发现每天下午三点左右，总有人来坐。有时候是带孩子的妈妈，有时候是看报纸的老人。大家都不说话，只是安静地坐着，享受那一小段阳光。我越来越觉得，一个好的地方不需要很大，也不需要很漂亮。它只需要在对的时间，给人一个可以停下来的理由。那张长椅比较旧了，但是它给了很多人一个安静的下午。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū de liǎng dòng lóu zhījiān, yǒu yì zhāng bèi rén wàng le de cháng yǐ. Chūntiān de shíhou, yángguāng gānghǎo zhào dào nàlǐ, nuǎnnuǎn de. Wǒ fāxiàn měi tiān xiàwǔ sān diǎn zuǒyòu, zǒng yǒu rén lái zuò. Yǒu shíhou shì dài háizi de māma, yǒu shíhou shì kàn bàozhǐ de lǎorén. Dàjiā dōu bù shuō huà, zhǐshì ānjìng de zuòzhe, xiǎngshòu nà yì xiǎo duàn yángguāng. Wǒ yuè lái yuè juéde, yí gè hǎo de dìfang bù xūyào hěn dà, yě bù xūyào hěn piàoliang. Tā zhǐ xūyào zài duì de shíjiān, gěi rén yí gè kěyǐ tíng xiàlái de lǐyóu. Nà zhāng cháng yǐ bǐjiào jiù le, dànshì tā gěi le hěn duō rén yí gè ānjìng de xiàwǔ.
text_en: Between two buildings in our complex, there's a bench everyone has forgotten about. In spring, the sunlight falls right there, warm and gentle. I've noticed that around three every afternoon, someone always comes to sit. Sometimes a mother with children, sometimes an old person reading the paper. Nobody talks — they just sit quietly, enjoying that small stretch of sunlight. I've come to feel more and more that a good place doesn't need to be big or beautiful. It just needs to give people a reason to pause at the right time. That bench is rather old, but it has given many people a quiet afternoon.

title: The Tailor's Measurements
text_zh: 小区门口的裁缝店已经开了二十年了。老板娘姓周，大家都叫她周阿姨。她量衣服的时候从来不用说话，手一碰你的肩膀，你就知道该站直了。她记得很多老客人的尺寸，有时候不量就能把衣服改好。有人说现在网上买衣服比较方便，不需要裁缝了。但是周阿姨说：「机器做的衣服是给大家穿的，我做的衣服是给你一个人穿的。」她的店越来越小了，但是来的人对她越来越信任。有些东西被时代替换了，但是有些东西越老越有价值。
text_pinyin: Xiǎoqū ménkǒu de cáiféng diàn yǐjīng kāi le èrshí nián le. Lǎobǎnniáng xìng Zhōu, dàjiā dōu jiào tā Zhōu āyí. Tā liáng yīfu de shíhou cónglái bú yòng shuō huà, shǒu yí pèng nǐ de jiānbǎng, nǐ jiù zhīdào gāi zhàn zhí le. Tā jìde hěn duō lǎo kèrén de chǐcun, yǒu shíhou bú liáng jiù néng bǎ yīfu gǎi hǎo. Yǒu rén shuō xiànzài wǎngshàng mǎi yīfu bǐjiào fāngbiàn, bù xūyào cáiféng le. Dànshì Zhōu āyí shuō:「Jīqì zuò de yīfu shì gěi dàjiā chuān de, wǒ zuò de yīfu shì gěi nǐ yí gè rén chuān de.」 Tā de diàn yuè lái yuè xiǎo le, dànshì lái de rén duì tā yuè lái yuè xìnrèn. Yǒuxiē dōngxi bèi shídài tìhuàn le, dànshì yǒuxiē dōngxi yuè lǎo yuè yǒu jiàzhí.
text_en: The tailor shop at the complex entrance has been open for twenty years. The owner's surname is Zhou — everyone calls her Auntie Zhou. When she measures, she never needs to speak; one touch on your shoulder and you know to stand straight. She remembers many regulars' measurements and can sometimes alter clothes without measuring. Some say buying online is more convenient, no need for tailors. But Auntie Zhou says: 'Machine-made clothes are for everyone. The clothes I make are for you alone.' Her shop keeps getting smaller, but her customers trust her more and more. Some things get replaced by the times, but some things become more valuable with age.

title: The Sound of Sweeping
text_zh: 每天早上五点半，楼下就会传来扫地的声音。一开始我觉得太早了，有点烦。但是慢慢地，那个声音变成了我的闹钟。有一天早上没有听到，我反而睡不着了，心里觉得少了什么。后来我才知道，扫地的阿姨生病了，休息了两天。她回来的那天早上，我听到扫地的声音，突然觉得很安心。有时候我们习惯了一个人的存在，只有当他不在的时候，才发现他对我们有多重要。那把扫帚的声音，比任何闹钟都让我觉得踏实。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang wǔ diǎn bàn, lóu xià jiù huì chuán lái sǎo dì de shēngyīn. Yì kāishǐ wǒ juéde tài zǎo le, yǒudiǎn fán. Dànshì mànman de, nàge shēngyīn biàn chéng le wǒ de nàozhōng. Yǒu yì tiān zǎoshang méiyǒu tīng dào, wǒ fǎn'ér shuì bù zháo le, xīnlǐ juéde shǎo le shénme. Hòulái wǒ cái zhīdào, sǎo dì de āyí shēngbìng le, xiūxi le liǎng tiān. Tā huílái de nà tiān zǎoshang, wǒ tīng dào sǎo dì de shēngyīn, tūrán juéde hěn ānxīn. Yǒu shíhou wǒmen xíguàn le yí gè rén de cúnzài, zhǐ yǒu dāng tā bú zài de shíhou, cái fāxiàn tā duì wǒmen yǒu duō zhòngyào. Nà bǎ sàozhou de shēngyīn, bǐ rènhé nàozhōng dōu ràng wǒ juéde tāshi.
text_en: Every morning at five thirty, the sound of sweeping drifts up from downstairs. At first I thought it was too early and found it annoying. But gradually, that sound became my alarm clock. One morning I didn't hear it and couldn't fall back asleep — something felt missing. Later I learned the sweeping auntie had gotten sick and rested for two days. The morning she came back, hearing the sweeping sound, I suddenly felt at ease. Sometimes we get used to someone's presence, and only when they're gone do we realize how important they are. That broom's sound makes me feel more grounded than any alarm clock.

title: The Window Washer's View
title_zh: 擦窗户的人看到的世界
text_zh: 我在二十楼的办公室上班。每个月有一天，窗户外面会出现一个擦窗户的人。他被绳子吊在半空中，一边擦一边慢慢地往下移。我总是忍不住看他，觉得他的工作很危险。有一次我把窗户打开，问他害不害怕。他说：「刚开始的时候害怕，但是后来发现，从这个角度看城市特别漂亮。你们每天在里面工作，看不到外面的样子。我虽然在外面，但是我看到的比你们多。」那天以后，我经常站在窗边往外看，试着用他的眼睛看这个城市。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài èrshí lóu de bàngōngshì shàng bān. Měi gè yuè yǒu yì tiān, chuānghu wàimiàn huì chūxiàn yí gè cā chuānghu de rén. Tā bèi shéngzi diào zài bàn kōng zhōng, yìbiān cā yìbiān mànman de wǎng xià yí. Wǒ zǒngshì rěn bú zhù kàn tā, juéde tā de gōngzuò hěn wēixiǎn. Yǒu yí cì wǒ bǎ chuānghu dǎkāi, wèn tā hài bú hàipà. Tā shuō:「Gāng kāishǐ de shíhou hàipà, dànshì hòulái fāxiàn, cóng zhège jiǎodù kàn chéngshì tèbié piàoliang. Nǐmen měi tiān zài lǐmiàn gōngzuò, kàn bú dào wàimiàn de yàngzi. Wǒ suīrán zài wàimiàn, dànshì wǒ kàn dào de bǐ nǐmen duō.」 Nà tiān yǐhòu, wǒ jīngcháng zhàn zài chuāng biān wǎng wài kàn, shìzhe yòng tā de yǎnjing kàn zhège chéngshì.
text_en: I work in an office on the twentieth floor. Once a month, a window washer appears outside. He hangs by ropes in midair, washing and slowly moving down. I always can't help watching, thinking his work is dangerous. Once I opened the window and asked if he was scared. He said: 'At first I was scared, but then I realized the city looks especially beautiful from this angle. You work inside every day and can't see what it looks like outside. I may be outside, but I see more than you do.' After that day, I often stand by the window looking out, trying to see the city through his eyes.

title: The Bookstore Cat
text_zh: 我常去的书店里有一只猫，是一只胖胖的橘色猫。它最喜欢睡在文学区的书架上。每次有人想拿它旁边的书，它就抬头看一眼，好像在说「你确定要看这本吗？」老板说这只猫是五年前自己跑来的，从那以后就没有离开过。有客人说被它挡住了，老板就笑着说：「它比我更了解哪些书值得看。」有时候下雨天，书店里没什么人，只有猫安安静静地趴在那里。那种画面让人觉得，世界上最温暖的地方，大概就是一家有猫的旧书店。
text_pinyin: Wǒ cháng qù de shūdiàn lǐ yǒu yì zhī māo, shì yì zhī pàngpàng de júsè māo. Tā zuì xǐhuan shuì zài wénxué qū de shūjià shàng. Měi cì yǒu rén xiǎng ná tā pángbiān de shū, tā jiù táitóu kàn yì yǎn, hǎoxiàng zài shuō「nǐ quèdìng yào kàn zhè běn ma?」 Lǎobǎn shuō zhè zhī māo shì wǔ nián qián zìjǐ pǎo lái de, cóng nà yǐhòu jiù méiyǒu líkāi guò. Yǒu kèrén shuō bèi tā dǎng zhù le, lǎobǎn jiù xiàozhe shuō:「Tā bǐ wǒ gèng liǎojiě nǎxiē shū zhíde kàn.」 Yǒu shíhou xià yǔ tiān, shūdiàn lǐ méi shénme rén, zhǐ yǒu māo ān'ānjìngjìng de pā zài nàlǐ. Nà zhǒng huàmiàn ràng rén juéde, shìjiè shàng zuì wēnnuǎn de dìfang, dàgài jiùshì yì jiā yǒu māo de jiù shūdiàn.
text_en: The bookstore I often visit has a cat — a chubby orange one. Its favorite spot is sleeping on the literature section shelf. Whenever someone reaches for a book beside it, it lifts its head and looks, as if saying 'are you sure you want this one?' The owner says the cat wandered in five years ago and never left. When customers say it's in the way, the owner laughs: 'It knows better than I do which books are worth reading.' Sometimes on rainy days, the store is almost empty, just the cat lying there peacefully. That scene makes you feel the warmest place in the world is probably an old bookstore with a cat.

title: The Alley That Wakes Up Last
text_zh: 在城市的中心，有一条很窄的巷子。跟旁边热闹的大街比较，这条巷子好像总是慢一拍。早上九点，大街上的人已经在忙了，但是巷子里的店还没有开门。十点左右，第一家早餐店才把桌子搬到外面。老板慢慢地擦桌子，一边跟隔壁的老板聊天。住在这里的人好像都不着急。他们说：「城市跑得太快了，我们这条巷子选择慢慢走。」每次走进这条巷子，我都觉得时间被放慢了。外面的世界还在跑，但是在这里，你可以停下来喝一碗粥，看一看天空。
text_pinyin: Zài chéngshì de zhōngxīn, yǒu yì tiáo hěn zhǎi de xiàngzi. Gēn pángbiān rènao de dà jiē bǐjiào, zhè tiáo xiàngzi hǎoxiàng zǒngshì màn yí pāi. Zǎoshang jiǔ diǎn, dà jiē shàng de rén yǐjīng zài máng le, dànshì xiàngzi lǐ de diàn hái méiyǒu kāi mén. Shí diǎn zuǒyòu, dì yī jiā zǎocān diàn cái bǎ zhuōzi bān dào wàimiàn. Lǎobǎn mànman de cā zhuōzi, yìbiān gēn gébì de lǎobǎn liáotiān. Zhù zài zhèlǐ de rén hǎoxiàng dōu bù zhāojí. Tāmen shuō:「Chéngshì pǎo de tài kuài le, wǒmen zhè tiáo xiàngzi xuǎnzé mànman zǒu.」 Měi cì zǒu jìn zhè tiáo xiàngzi, wǒ dōu juéde shíjiān bèi fàng màn le. Wàimiàn de shìjiè hái zài pǎo, dànshì zài zhèlǐ, nǐ kěyǐ tíng xiàlái hē yì wǎn zhōu, kàn yi kàn tiānkōng.
text_en: In the center of the city, there's a very narrow alley. Compared to the bustling main street beside it, this alley always seems one beat behind. At nine in the morning, people on the main street are already busy, but shops in the alley haven't opened yet. Around ten, the first breakfast shop finally moves its tables outside. The owner slowly wipes the tables while chatting with the shop next door. People who live here never seem to be in a hurry. They say: 'The city runs too fast — our alley chooses to walk slowly.' Every time I walk into this alley, I feel time has been slowed down. The outside world is still running, but here you can stop to drink a bowl of porridge and look at the sky.

title: The Rooftop Garden
text_zh: 我家楼顶有一个天台。以前那里什么都没有，只有灰尘和旧东西。后来六楼的王奶奶把一些旧盆子搬上去，开始种菜。一开始只种了几棵葱。慢慢地，西红柿、黄瓜、辣椒都有了。现在整个天台变成了一个小菜园。邻居们越来越感兴趣，有人帮忙浇水，有人带来了新的种子。王奶奶把种出来的菜分给大家。以前大家住在一栋楼里都不说话，现在因为这个菜园，邻居们开始互相认识了。一个小小的天台，被一个老人变成了整栋楼最温暖的地方。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóudǐng yǒu yí gè tiāntái. Yǐqián nàlǐ shénme dōu méiyǒu, zhǐ yǒu huīchén hé jiù dōngxi. Hòulái liù lóu de Wáng nǎinai bǎ yìxiē jiù pénzi bān shàngqù, kāishǐ zhòng cài. Yì kāishǐ zhǐ zhòng le jǐ kē cōng. Mànman de, xīhóngshì, huángguā, làjiāo dōu yǒu le. Xiànzài zhěng gè tiāntái biàn chéng le yí gè xiǎo càiyuán. Línjūmen yuè lái yuè gǎn xìngqù, yǒu rén bāngmáng jiāo shuǐ, yǒu rén dài lái le xīn de zhǒngzi. Wáng nǎinai bǎ zhòng chūlái de cài fēn gěi dàjiā. Yǐqián dàjiā zhù zài yí dòng lóu lǐ dōu bù shuō huà, xiànzài yīnwèi zhège càiyuán, línjūmen kāishǐ hùxiāng rènshi le. Yí gè xiǎoxiǎo de tiāntái, bèi yí gè lǎorén biàn chéng le zhěng dòng lóu zuì wēnnuǎn de dìfang.
text_en: My building's rooftop has a terrace. Before, nothing was there — just dust and old things. Then Grandma Wang from the sixth floor carried old pots up and started growing vegetables. At first just a few green onions. Gradually, tomatoes, cucumbers, and peppers appeared. Now the whole terrace has become a small garden. Neighbors grew more and more interested — some help water, some bring new seeds. Grandma Wang shares what she grows with everyone. Before, people in the same building never talked; now because of this garden, neighbors have started getting to know each other. A small terrace was turned by one old person into the warmest place in the whole building.

title: The Underground Musician
text_zh: 地铁站和商场之间有一条地下通道。每天下班的时候，那里都站着一个拉小提琴的男人。他穿得很干净，眼睛闭着，身体跟着音乐轻轻地摇。大部分人走过去的时候不会停下来，但是他好像并不在乎。有一天我停下来听了一首完整的曲子。他拉完以后睁开眼睛看到我，微微笑了一下。我往他的琴盒里放了钱。他说：「谢谢你愿意停下来。其实我不是为了钱——我只是想让走过的人的心情好一点。」从那以后，我每次经过都会放慢脚步，让那段音乐陪我走一小段路。
text_pinyin: Dìtiě zhàn hé shāngchǎng zhījiān yǒu yì tiáo dìxià tōngdào. Měi tiān xiàbān de shíhou, nàlǐ dōu zhànzhe yí gè lā xiǎotíqín de nánrén. Tā chuān de hěn gānjìng, yǎnjing bìzhe, shēntǐ gēnzhe yīnyuè qīngqīng de yáo. Dà bùfen rén zǒu guòqù de shíhou bú huì tíng xiàlái, dànshì tā hǎoxiàng bìng bú zàihū. Yǒu yì tiān wǒ tíng xiàlái tīng le yì shǒu wánzhěng de qǔzi. Tā lā wán yǐhòu zhēng kāi yǎnjing kàn dào wǒ, wēiwēi xiào le yíxià. Wǒ wǎng tā de qínhé lǐ fàng le qián. Tā shuō:「Xièxie nǐ yuànyì tíng xiàlái. Qíshí wǒ búshì wèile qián——wǒ zhǐshì xiǎng ràng zǒu guò de rén de xīnqíng hǎo yìdiǎn.」 Cóng nà yǐhòu, wǒ měi cì jīngguò dōu huì fàng màn jiǎobù, ràng nà duàn yīnyuè péi wǒ zǒu yì xiǎo duàn lù.
text_en: Between the subway station and the mall, there's an underground passage. Every evening at rush hour, a man stands there playing the violin. He dresses neatly, eyes closed, body gently swaying with the music. Most people pass without stopping, but he doesn't seem to mind. One day I stopped and listened to a complete piece. When he finished and opened his eyes, he saw me and gave a small smile. I put money in his case. He said: 'Thank you for being willing to stop. Actually I'm not doing this for money — I just want people passing by to feel a little better.' Since then, every time I pass I slow my steps and let that music walk with me a short way.

title: The Corner Mailbox
text_zh: 我家附近有一个绿色的老信箱。现在大家都用手机发信息了，但是那个信箱还在。有一天我看到一个小女孩站在信箱前面，踮着脚往里面塞了一封信。她的妈妈站在旁边笑。我走过去问她寄给谁。小女孩说：「寄给圣诞老人。」她妈妈小声对我说：「其实信寄不出去，但是我不想告诉她。让她相信一些美好的事情，再多一年也好。」我突然觉得，那个旧信箱虽然不再被用来寄信了，但是它装着一个孩子的梦。有些东西的价值不在于它还有没有用，而在于它还能不能承载人们的感情。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn yǒu yí gè lǜsè de lǎo xìnxiāng. Xiànzài dàjiā dōu yòng shǒujī fā xìnxī le, dànshì nàge xìnxiāng hái zài. Yǒu yì tiān wǒ kàn dào yí gè xiǎo nǚhái zhàn zài xìnxiāng qiánmiàn, diǎnzhe jiǎo wǎng lǐmiàn sāi le yì fēng xìn. Tā de māma zhàn zài pángbiān xiào. Wǒ zǒu guòqù wèn tā jì gěi shéi. Xiǎo nǚhái shuō:「Jì gěi Shèngdàn lǎorén.」 Tā māma xiǎo shēng duì wǒ shuō:「Qíshí xìn jì bù chūqù, dànshì wǒ bù xiǎng gàosu tā. Ràng tā xiāngxìn yìxiē měihǎo de shìqing, zài duō yì nián yě hǎo.」 Wǒ tūrán juéde, nàge jiù xìnxiāng suīrán bú zài bèi yòng lái jì xìn le, dànshì tā zhuāngzhe yí gè háizi de mèng. Yǒuxiē dōngxi de jiàzhí bú zàiyú tā hái yǒu méiyǒu yòng, ér zàiyú tā hái néng bù néng chéngzài rénmen de gǎnqíng.
text_en: Near my home there's a green old mailbox. Everyone uses phones to send messages now, but that mailbox is still there. One day I saw a little girl standing before it, on tiptoe, pushing a letter inside. Her mother stood nearby, smiling. I walked over and asked who the letter was for. The girl said: 'Santa Claus.' Her mother whispered to me: 'Actually the letter won't get delivered, but I don't want to tell her. Letting her believe in something beautiful for one more year is fine.' I suddenly felt that although the old mailbox is no longer used for sending letters, it holds a child's dream. The value of some things isn't whether they're still useful, but whether they can still carry people's feelings.

title: The Night Bus Home
text_zh: 晚上十一点半的末班车上，人不多。每个人都很累，有的人靠着窗户睡着了，有的人戴着耳机闭着眼睛。司机开得很稳，好像知道车上的人需要安静。车窗外面是一排排的路灯，一闪一闪地往后退。我喜欢坐在最后一排，看着前面那些疲惫的身影。虽然我们谁都不认识，但是在这辆车上，我们都是一样的——都是想回家的人。末班车比较慢，但是没有人抱怨。大概是因为大家都知道，不管多晚，只要还有一辆车，就能到家。
text_pinyin: Wǎnshang shíyī diǎn bàn de mòbānchē shàng, rén bù duō. Měi gè rén dōu hěn lèi, yǒu de rén kàozhe chuānghu shuìzháo le, yǒu de rén dàizhe ěrjī bìzhe yǎnjing. Sījī kāi de hěn wěn, hǎoxiàng zhīdào chē shàng de rén xūyào ānjìng. Chēchuāng wàimiàn shì yì pái pái de lùdēng, yí shǎn yí shǎn de wǎng hòu tuì. Wǒ xǐhuan zuò zài zuìhòu yì pái, kànzhe qiánmiàn nàxiē píbèi de shēnyǐng. Suīrán wǒmen shéi dōu bú rènshi, dànshì zài zhè liàng chē shàng, wǒmen dōu shì yíyàng de——dōu shì xiǎng huí jiā de rén. Mòbānchē bǐjiào màn, dànshì méiyǒu rén bàoyuàn. Dàgài shì yīnwèi dàjiā dōu zhīdào, bùguǎn duō wǎn, zhǐ yào hái yǒu yí liàng chē, jiù néng dào jiā.
text_en: On the last bus at eleven thirty, there aren't many people. Everyone is tired — some lean against windows asleep, some wear earphones with eyes closed. The driver drives steadily, as if knowing the passengers need quiet. Outside the windows, rows of streetlights flash past one by one. I like sitting in the last row, watching the tired silhouettes ahead. Although none of us know each other, on this bus we're all the same — all people who want to get home. The last bus is rather slow, but no one complains. Probably because everyone knows that no matter how late it is, as long as there's still one bus running, you can get home.

title: The Hospital Cafeteria at 3 AM
text_zh: 医院的食堂二十四小时都开着。凌晨三点的时候，里面只有几个人。有刚做完手术的医生，手还在轻轻地抖。有在走廊里等了一整夜的家属，眼睛红红的。食堂阿姨给每个人端上热粥，不多问。她说：「在这个时间来吃饭的人，都有自己的难处。我能做的就是让粥热一点。」我爸爸住院的那几天，我每天凌晨都来这里。那碗粥的味道很普通，但是在那个时候，它比任何东西都温暖。有些安慰不需要说出来，一碗热粥就够了。
text_pinyin: Yīyuàn de shítáng èrshísì xiǎoshí dōu kāizhe. Língchén sān diǎn de shíhou, lǐmiàn zhǐ yǒu jǐ gè rén. Yǒu gāng zuò wán shǒushù de yīshēng, shǒu hái zài qīngqīng de dǒu. Yǒu zài zǒuláng lǐ děng le yì zhěng yè de jiāshǔ, yǎnjing hónghóng de. Shítáng āyí gěi měi gè rén duān shàng rè zhōu, bù duō wèn. Tā shuō:「Zài zhège shíjiān lái chī fàn de rén, dōu yǒu zìjǐ de nánchù. Wǒ néng zuò de jiùshì ràng zhōu rè yìdiǎn.」 Wǒ bàba zhùyuàn de nà jǐ tiān, wǒ měi tiān língchén dōu lái zhèlǐ. Nà wǎn zhōu de wèidào hěn pǔtōng, dànshì zài nàge shíhou, tā bǐ rènhé dōngxi dōu wēnnuǎn. Yǒuxiē ānwèi bù xūyào shuō chūlái, yì wǎn rè zhōu jiù gòu le.
text_en: The hospital cafeteria stays open twenty-four hours. At three in the morning, only a few people are inside. A doctor who just finished surgery, hands still trembling slightly. Family members who waited in the corridor all night, eyes red. The cafeteria auntie brings everyone hot porridge without asking much. She says: 'People who come to eat at this hour all have their own hardships. All I can do is make the porridge a little hotter.' During the days my father was hospitalized, I came here every early morning. That porridge tasted ordinary, but at that moment, it was warmer than anything. Some comfort doesn't need words — a bowl of hot porridge is enough.

title: The School Gate Guard
text_zh: 我们学校门口有一个保安，姓刘，大家叫他刘叔。他每天早上六点半就站在门口，不管刮风下雨。学生们进门的时候，他会跟每个人点头。毕业那天，很多学生跑去跟他合影。他红着脸说：「我就是个看门的，有什么好拍的。」但是一个学生对他说：「刘叔，你是我们四年里每天第一个看到的人。」他听了以后，眼睛一下子就红了。有些人虽然不教你知识，但是他们的存在本身就是一种教育——关于坚持，关于认真，关于每天早起站在那里等你来。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xuéxiào ménkǒu yǒu yí gè bǎo'ān, xìng Liú, dàjiā jiào tā Liú shū. Tā měi tiān zǎoshang liù diǎn bàn jiù zhàn zài ménkǒu, bùguǎn guā fēng xià yǔ. Xuéshēngmen jìn mén de shíhou, tā huì gēn měi gè rén diǎntóu. Bìyè nà tiān, hěn duō xuéshēng pǎo qù gēn tā héyǐng. Tā hóngzhe liǎn shuō:「Wǒ jiùshì ge kān mén de, yǒu shénme hǎo pāi de.」 Dànshì yí gè xuéshēng duì tā shuō:「Liú shū, nǐ shì wǒmen sì nián lǐ měi tiān dì yī gè kàn dào de rén.」 Tā tīng le yǐhòu, yǎnjing yíxiàzi jiù hóng le. Yǒuxiē rén suīrán bù jiāo nǐ zhīshi, dànshì tāmen de cúnzài běnshēn jiùshì yì zhǒng jiàoyù——guānyú jiānchí, guānyú rènzhēn, guānyú měi tiān zǎo qǐ zhàn zài nàlǐ děng nǐ lái.
text_en: Our school entrance has a security guard surnamed Liu — everyone calls him Uncle Liu. He stands at the gate every morning at six thirty, rain or shine. When students enter, he nods to each one. On graduation day, many students ran to take photos with him. Blushing, he said: 'I'm just a gatekeeper, what's worth photographing?' But one student told him: 'Uncle Liu, you're the first person we see every day for four years.' After hearing that, his eyes turned red instantly. Some people don't teach you knowledge, but their very presence is a kind of education — about persistence, about dedication, about waking up early every day to stand there and wait for you.

questions.q_en: What does Uncle Liu do every morning?

options.pinyin: liù diǎn bàn zhàn zài ménkǒu

questions.q_zh: 毕业那天，学生对刘叔说了什么？

title: The Library's Quiet Rule
text_zh: 我们城市的图书馆有一个特别的规则：每天下午三点到四点是「完全安静时间」。在这一个小时里，不能说话，不能打电话，连翻书的声音都要尽量小。一开始很多人觉得太严格了。但是慢慢地，越来越多的人专门为了这一个小时来图书馆。有人说：「在外面找不到安静，只有这里可以。」图书馆的管理员说：「现在的世界太吵了。我们不能让整个世界安静下来，但是我们可以保护一个小时的安静。」我觉得这个规则比较特别，因为它保护的不是书，而是人需要安静的权利。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen chéngshì de túshūguǎn yǒu yí gè tèbié de guīzé: měi tiān xiàwǔ sān diǎn dào sì diǎn shì「wánquán ānjìng shíjiān」. Zài zhè yí gè xiǎoshí lǐ, bù néng shuō huà, bù néng dǎ diànhuà, lián fān shū de shēngyīn dōu yào jǐnliàng xiǎo. Yì kāishǐ hěn duō rén juéde tài yángé le. Dànshì mànman de, yuè lái yuè duō de rén zhuānmén wèile zhè yí gè xiǎoshí lái túshūguǎn. Yǒu rén shuō:「Zài wàimiàn zhǎo bú dào ānjìng, zhǐ yǒu zhèlǐ kěyǐ.」 Túshūguǎn de guǎnlǐyuán shuō:「Xiànzài de shìjiè tài chǎo le. Wǒmen bù néng ràng zhěng gè shìjiè ānjìng xiàlái, dànshì wǒmen kěyǐ bǎohù yí gè xiǎoshí de ānjìng.」 Wǒ juéde zhège guīzé bǐjiào tèbié, yīnwèi tā bǎohù de búshì shū, érshì rén xūyào ānjìng de quánlì.
text_en: Our city's library has a special rule: every afternoon from three to four is 'complete quiet time.' During this hour, no talking, no phone calls, and even the sound of turning pages should be as soft as possible. At first many thought it was too strict. But gradually, more and more people come specifically for this hour. Someone said: 'I can't find quiet anywhere else — only here.' The librarian says: 'The world is too noisy now. We can't make the whole world quiet, but we can protect one hour of silence.' I think this rule is special because it doesn't protect books — it protects people's right to quiet.

title: The Post Office Counter
text_zh: 城东有一个很小的邮局，只有两个窗口。每次去都要排很长的队。但是我还是喜欢去那里寄东西，因为里面的工作人员特别有耐心。有一次，一个老奶奶不会填单子，工作人员就一笔一笔地教她写。后面排队的人也没有人催。大家都安安静静地等着。我觉得这个邮局虽然又小又慢，但是它让我想起了一种比较老的生活方式——人们不着急，愿意等，也愿意帮别人。现在这样的地方越来越少了，所以每次去都让我觉得特别珍贵。
text_pinyin: Chéng dōng yǒu yí gè hěn xiǎo de yóujú, zhǐ yǒu liǎng gè chuāngkǒu. Měi cì qù dōu yào pái hěn cháng de duì. Dànshì wǒ háishi xǐhuan qù nàlǐ jì dōngxi, yīnwèi lǐmiàn de gōngzuò rényuán tèbié yǒu nàixīn. Yǒu yí cì, yí gè lǎo nǎinai bú huì tián dānzi, gōngzuò rényuán jiù yì bǐ yì bǐ de jiāo tā xiě. Hòumiàn páiduì de rén yě méiyǒu rén cuī. Dàjiā dōu ān'ānjìngjìng de děngzhe. Wǒ juéde zhège yóujú suīrán yòu xiǎo yòu màn, dànshì tā ràng wǒ xiǎng qǐ le yì zhǒng bǐjiào lǎo de shēnghuó fāngshì——rénmen bù zhāojí, yuànyì děng, yě yuànyì bāng biéren. Xiànzài zhèyàng de dìfang yuè lái yuè shǎo le, suǒyǐ měi cì qù dōu ràng wǒ juéde tèbié zhēnguì.
text_en: In the east of the city there's a very small post office with only two windows. There's always a long line. But I still like going there to mail things, because the staff are especially patient. Once, an old grandmother couldn't fill out a form, and the worker taught her stroke by stroke. Nobody in line behind them rushed. Everyone waited quietly. I think although this post office is small and slow, it reminds me of an older way of living — people weren't in a hurry, were willing to wait, and willing to help others. Places like this are becoming fewer, so every visit feels especially precious.

title: The Neighbor's Light
text_zh: 对面楼的一扇窗户，每天晚上十一点准时亮灯，凌晨两点准时关灯。三个小时，从来没有变过。我很好奇里面住着什么人。有一天我忍不住问了楼下的阿姨。她说那是一个退休的老教授，每天晚上都在写一本关于本地方言的书。他已经写了五年了，还没有写完。我突然觉得，那扇窗户后面不是一个人在熬夜，而是一个人在安安静静地做一件他觉得重要的事。那盏灯不只是灯——它是一种坚持发出的光。从那以后，每天晚上看到那扇灯亮着，我的心里就会平静一些。
text_pinyin: Duìmiàn lóu de yì shàn chuānghu, měi tiān wǎnshang shíyī diǎn zhǔnshí liàng dēng, língchén liǎng diǎn zhǔnshí guān dēng. Sān gè xiǎoshí, cónglái méiyǒu biàn guò. Wǒ hěn hàoqí lǐmiàn zhùzhe shénme rén. Yǒu yì tiān wǒ rěn bú zhù wèn le lóu xià de āyí. Tā shuō nà shì yí gè tuìxiū de lǎo jiàoshòu, měi tiān wǎnshang dōu zài xiě yì běn guānyú běndì fāngyán de shū. Tā yǐjīng xiě le wǔ nián le, hái méiyǒu xiě wán. Wǒ tūrán juéde, nà shàn chuānghu hòumiàn búshì yí gè rén zài áo yè, érshì yí gè rén zài ān'ānjìngjìng de zuò yí jiàn tā juéde zhòngyào de shì. Nà zhǎn dēng bù zhǐshì dēng——tā shì yì zhǒng jiānchí fāchū de guāng. Cóng nà yǐhòu, měi tiān wǎnshang kàn dào nà shàn dēng liàngzhe, wǒ de xīnlǐ jiù huì píngjìng yìxiē.
text_en: A window in the building across from mine turns on at exactly eleven every night and off at exactly two in the morning. Three hours, never changing. I was curious about who lived there. One day I couldn't help asking the auntie downstairs. She said it's a retired old professor who writes a book about the local dialect every night. He's been writing for five years and hasn't finished yet. I suddenly realized that behind that window isn't someone staying up too late — it's someone quietly doing something they believe is important. That lamp isn't just a lamp — it's the light of persistence. Since then, seeing that light on every night makes my heart a little calmer.

title: The Piano in the Empty Room
text_zh: 我楼上的房子已经空了半年了。但是每个周末下午，我都能听到钢琴声从上面传下来。一开始我以为是有人在播放音乐，但是声音很真实，有时候还会弹错，然后停下来重新开始。我问物业，他们说那个房子确实没有人住。有一天周末我上去看了看，门没有锁。推开门以后，我看到一个十几岁的女孩坐在一架旧钢琴前面。她说她家里没有钢琴，她发现这个空房子里有一架，就每个周末来练习。她的眼睛里有一种安静的认真。我没有告诉物业。有些秘密是美好的，不需要被打破。
text_pinyin: Wǒ lóu shàng de fángzi yǐjīng kōng le bàn nián le. Dànshì měi gè zhōumò xiàwǔ, wǒ dōu néng tīng dào gāngqín shēng cóng shàngmiàn chuán xiàlái. Yì kāishǐ wǒ yǐwéi shì yǒu rén zài bōfàng yīnyuè, dànshì shēngyīn hěn zhēnshí, yǒu shíhou hái huì tán cuò, ránhòu tíng xiàlái chóngxīn kāishǐ. Wǒ wèn wùyè, tāmen shuō nàge fángzi quèshí méiyǒu rén zhù. Yǒu yì tiān zhōumò wǒ shàngqù kàn le kàn, mén méiyǒu suǒ. Tuī kāi mén yǐhòu, wǒ kàn dào yí gè shí jǐ suì de nǚhái zuò zài yí jià jiù gāngqín qiánmiàn. Tā shuō tā jiā lǐ méiyǒu gāngqín, tā fāxiàn zhège kōng fángzi lǐ yǒu yí jià, jiù měi gè zhōumò lái liànxí. Tā de yǎnjing lǐ yǒu yì zhǒng ānjìng de rènzhēn. Wǒ méiyǒu gàosu wùyè. Yǒuxiē mìmì shì měihǎo de, bù xūyào bèi dǎpò.
text_en: The apartment above mine has been empty for half a year. But every weekend afternoon, I hear piano music drifting down. At first I thought someone was playing a recording, but the sound was too real — sometimes there were wrong notes, then pauses to start over. I asked property management; they confirmed no one lives there. One weekend I went up to look. The door wasn't locked. Pushing it open, I saw a teenage girl sitting at an old piano. She said she doesn't have a piano at home and discovered this empty room had one, so she comes every weekend to practice. Her eyes held a quiet seriousness. I didn't tell property management. Some secrets are beautiful and don't need to be broken.

title: The Coat on the Park Bench
text_zh: 冬天的一个早上，我在公园跑步的时候看到长椅上放着一件干净的外套。我以为是谁忘了的。第二天，外套不见了。但是第三天早上，又出现了一件不同的外套。我开始注意，发现每隔一两天就会有一件外套出现在那里。有一天早上我起得特别早，终于看到了——一个中年男人把一件外套轻轻放在长椅上，然后就走了。我跑过去问他。他说：「冬天太冷了，公园里有些流浪的人没有外套。我把家里不穿的衣服洗干净放在这里。不用说是谁给的——需要的人自己会拿。」他笑了笑，消失在了早晨的雾里。
text_pinyin: Dōngtiān de yí gè zǎoshang, wǒ zài gōngyuán pǎobù de shíhou kàn dào cháng yǐ shàng fàngzhe yí jiàn gānjìng de wàitào. Wǒ yǐwéi shì shéi wàng le de. Dì èr tiān, wàitào bú jiàn le. Dànshì dì sān tiān zǎoshang, yòu chūxiàn le yí jiàn bùtóng de wàitào. Wǒ kāishǐ zhùyì, fāxiàn měi gé yì liǎng tiān jiù huì yǒu yí jiàn wàitào chūxiàn zài nàlǐ. Yǒu yì tiān zǎoshang wǒ qǐ de tèbié zǎo, zhōngyú kàn dào le——yí gè zhōngnián nánrén bǎ yí jiàn wàitào qīngqīng fàng zài cháng yǐ shàng, ránhòu jiù zǒu le. Wǒ pǎo guòqù wèn tā. Tā shuō:「Dōngtiān tài lěng le, gōngyuán lǐ yǒuxiē liúlàng de rén méiyǒu wàitào. Wǒ bǎ jiā lǐ bù chuān de yīfu xǐ gānjìng fàng zài zhèlǐ. Bú yòng shuō shì shéi gěi de——xūyào de rén zìjǐ huì ná.」 Tā xiào le xiào, xiāoshī zài le zǎochén de wù lǐ.
text_en: One winter morning while jogging in the park, I saw a clean coat on the bench. I thought someone forgot it. The next day it was gone. But the third morning, a different coat appeared. I started paying attention and found that every day or two, a coat would appear there. One morning I woke up very early and finally saw — a middle-aged man gently placed a coat on the bench and walked away. I ran over to ask him. He said: 'Winter is too cold, and some homeless people in the park don't have coats. I wash clothes we don't wear at home and leave them here. No need to say who gave them — people who need them will take them.' He smiled and disappeared into the morning fog.

title: The Handwriting That Changed
text_zh: 整理旧东西的时候，我找到了一摞以前写的日记。翻开第一页，字写得歪歪扭扭的，像小孩画画。那时候我刚开始学中文，每个字都要写好几遍。再往后翻，字越来越好看了，句子也越来越长。最后几页是去年写的，已经能写整段的想法了。我把这些日记从头到尾看了一遍，觉得好像在看一个人慢慢长大的过程。那些歪歪扭扭的字，现在看起来比后面写得好的字更让我感动。因为那是我最勇敢的时候——什么都不会，但是愿意开始。
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ jiù dōngxi de shíhou, wǒ zhǎo dào le yì luò yǐqián xiě de rìjì. Fān kāi dì yī yè, zì xiě de wāiwāiniǔniǔ de, xiàng xiǎohái huà huà. Nà shíhou wǒ gāng kāishǐ xué Zhōngwén, měi gè zì dōu yào xiě hǎo jǐ biàn. Zài wǎng hòu fān, zì yuè lái yuè hǎokàn le, jùzi yě yuè lái yuè cháng. Zuìhòu jǐ yè shì qùnián xiě de, yǐjīng néng xiě zhěng duàn de xiǎngfa le. Wǒ bǎ zhèxiē rìjì cóng tóu dào wěi kàn le yí biàn, juéde hǎoxiàng zài kàn yí gè rén mànman zhǎng dà de guòchéng. Nàxiē wāiwāiniǔniǔ de zì, xiànzài kàn qǐlái bǐ hòumiàn xiě de hǎo de zì gèng ràng wǒ gǎndòng. Yīnwèi nà shì wǒ zuì yǒnggǎn de shíhou——shénme dōu bú huì, dànshì yuànyì kāishǐ.
text_en: While sorting old things, I found a stack of diaries I wrote before. Opening the first page, the characters were crooked and messy, like a child's drawing. Back then I had just started learning Chinese, writing each character several times over. Flipping further, the characters got better and the sentences longer. The last pages were from last year — I could already write full paragraphs of thoughts. Reading these diaries from start to finish felt like watching someone slowly grow up. Those crooked characters move me more now than the better writing that came later. Because that was my bravest time — knowing nothing, but willing to begin.

title: Two Ways of Saying Sorry
text_zh: 我发现，说中文的时候说「对不起」和说英文的时候说 sorry 感觉不一样。说 sorry 的时候，我觉得比较轻松，因为从小就习惯了。但是说「对不起」的时候，我觉得更认真，好像把错误看得更重了。我问我的中国朋友，他说：「因为『对不起』这三个字的意思是『我对不起你』——我做了对你不好的事。它比 sorry 更沉。」我想了想，觉得他说得对。语言不只是翻译，每个词背后都有不一样的重量。学一种新语言，不只是学新的词，而是学一种新的感受方式。
text_pinyin: Wǒ fāxiàn, shuō Zhōngwén de shíhou shuō「duìbuqǐ」hé shuō Yīngwén de shíhou shuō sorry gǎnjué bù yíyàng. Shuō sorry de shíhou, wǒ juéde bǐjiào qīngsōng, yīnwèi cóng xiǎo jiù xíguàn le. Dànshì shuō「duìbuqǐ」de shíhou, wǒ juéde gèng rènzhēn, hǎoxiàng bǎ cuòwù kàn de gèng zhòng le. Wǒ wèn wǒ de Zhōngguó péngyou, tā shuō:「Yīnwèi 'duìbuqǐ' zhè sān gè zì de yìsi shì 'wǒ duì bù qǐ nǐ'——wǒ zuò le duì nǐ bù hǎo de shì. Tā bǐ sorry gèng chén.」 Wǒ xiǎng le xiǎng, juéde tā shuō de duì. Yǔyán bù zhǐshì fānyì, měi gè cí bèihòu dōu yǒu bù yíyàng de zhòngliàng. Xué yì zhǒng xīn yǔyán, bù zhǐshì xué xīn de cí, érshì xué yì zhǒng xīn de gǎnshòu fāngshì.
text_en: I've noticed that saying 'duibuqi' in Chinese and 'sorry' in English feel different. When I say sorry, I feel more relaxed because I've been used to it since childhood. But saying 'duibuqi' feels more serious, as if the mistake weighs heavier. I asked my Chinese friend. He said: 'Because these three characters literally mean I have let you down — I did something bad to you. It's heavier than sorry.' I thought about it and realized he was right. Language isn't just translation — every word carries a different weight behind it. Learning a new language isn't just learning new words, but learning a new way of feeling.

title: The Photograph Without Me
text_zh: 朋友发了一张旅行的合影，里面有我们六个人。但是我看了很久才找到自己——因为我站在最后面，被别人挡住了一半。我一开始觉得有点不高兴。但是后来想了想，我其实一直都是这样的人：不喜欢站在前面，不喜欢被注意到。在聚会上我是安静听别人说话的那个。在团队里我是默默做事的那个。有人说这样不好，应该更主动。但是我觉得，不是每个人都需要站在最前面。有些人的价值，不需要被看到才存在。就像那张照片里的我——虽然被挡住了，但是我知道自己在那里，这就够了。
text_pinyin: Péngyou fā le yì zhāng lǚxíng de héyǐng, lǐmiàn yǒu wǒmen liù gè rén. Dànshì wǒ kàn le hěn jiǔ cái zhǎo dào zìjǐ——yīnwèi wǒ zhàn zài zuì hòumiàn, bèi biéren dǎng zhù le yíbàn. Wǒ yì kāishǐ juéde yǒudiǎn bù gāoxìng. Dànshì hòulái xiǎng le xiǎng, wǒ qíshí yìzhí dōu shì zhèyàng de rén: bù xǐhuan zhàn zài qiánmiàn, bù xǐhuan bèi zhùyì dào. Zài jùhuì shàng wǒ shì ānjìng tīng biéren shuō huà de nàge. Zài tuánduì lǐ wǒ shì mòmò zuòshì de nàge. Yǒu rén shuō zhèyàng bù hǎo, yīnggāi gèng zhǔdòng. Dànshì wǒ juéde, búshì měi gè rén dōu xūyào zhàn zài zuì qiánmiàn. Yǒuxiē rén de jiàzhí, bù xūyào bèi kàn dào cái cúnzài. Jiù xiàng nà zhāng zhàopiàn lǐ de wǒ——suīrán bèi dǎng zhù le, dànshì wǒ zhīdào zìjǐ zài nàlǐ, zhè jiù gòu le.
text_en: A friend posted a group photo from a trip with six of us. But I looked a long time before finding myself — I was standing in the very back, half hidden behind others. At first I felt a little unhappy. But then I thought about it: I've actually always been this kind of person — don't like standing in front, don't like being noticed. At gatherings I'm the one quietly listening. On teams I'm the one silently getting things done. Some say that's not good, that I should be more proactive. But I think not everyone needs to stand at the very front. Some people's value doesn't need to be seen to exist. Just like me in that photo — though I'm hidden, I know I'm there, and that's enough.

title: The Wrong WeChat Voice Message
text_zh: 昨天我在微信上给妈妈发了一条语音：「妈，今天加班，不回家吃饭了。」但是我发错人了——发给了我的老板。更糟糕的是，我老板回复了一条语音：「好的，那我给你留饭。」全办公室的人都笑了。后来老板说他是故意开玩笑的。但是从那以后，每次我加班到很晚，他真的会给我带一份晚饭。我跟同事说这件事，同事说：「所以发错消息有时候也是好事？」我想了想说：「大概是因为我老板比较像我妈。」
text_pinyin: Zuótiān wǒ zài Wēixìn shàng gěi māma fā le yì tiáo yǔyīn:「Mā, jīntiān jiābān, bù huí jiā chī fàn le.」 Dànshì wǒ fā cuò rén le——fā gěi le wǒ de lǎobǎn. Gèng zāogāo de shì, wǒ lǎobǎn huífù le yì tiáo yǔyīn:「Hǎo de, nà wǒ gěi nǐ liú fàn.」 Quán bàngōngshì de rén dōu xiào le. Hòulái lǎobǎn shuō tā shì gùyì kāi wánxiào de. Dànshì cóng nà yǐhòu, měi cì wǒ jiābān dào hěn wǎn, tā zhēn de huì gěi wǒ dài yí fèn wǎnfàn. Wǒ gēn tóngshì shuō zhè jiàn shì, tóngshì shuō:「Suǒyǐ fā cuò xiāoxi yǒu shíhou yě shì hǎo shì?」 Wǒ xiǎng le xiǎng shuō:「Dàgài shì yīnwèi wǒ lǎobǎn bǐjiào xiàng wǒ mā.」
text_en: Yesterday I sent a voice message on WeChat to my mom: 'Mom, working overtime today, won't come home for dinner.' But I sent it to the wrong person — my boss. Worse, my boss replied with a voice message: 'Okay, I'll save you some food.' The whole office laughed. Later the boss said he was joking on purpose. But since then, every time I work overtime late, he actually brings me dinner. I told my colleague about it. My colleague said: 'So sending to the wrong person is sometimes a good thing?' I thought about it and said: 'Probably because my boss is a lot like my mom.'

title: The Delivery Driver's Review
text_zh: 我点了一份外卖，备注写着「请帮我多放一点辣」。外卖到了以后，我发现里面放了一张小纸条：「已经放了三倍的辣，如果你还觉得不够辣，我建议你直接吃辣椒。」我笑了好久。后来我给他写了一个五星好评：「辣得我哭了，但是纸条让我笑了。」第二天我又点了同一家店，备注写着「还是那个爱吃辣的人」。这次纸条上写着：「又是你！这次放了四倍。别再哭了。」我觉得，有时候让一天变好的不是什么大事，而是一个陌生人跟你开的一个小玩笑。
text_pinyin: Wǒ diǎn le yí fèn wàimài, bèizhù xiězhe「qǐng bāng wǒ duō fàng yìdiǎn là」. Wàimài dào le yǐhòu, wǒ fāxiàn lǐmiàn fàng le yì zhāng xiǎo zhǐtiáo:「Yǐjīng fàng le sān bèi de là, rúguǒ nǐ hái juéde bú gòu là, wǒ jiànyì nǐ zhíjiē chī làjiāo.」 Wǒ xiào le hǎo jiǔ. Hòulái wǒ gěi tā xiě le yí gè wǔ xīng hǎopíng:「Là de wǒ kū le, dànshì zhǐtiáo ràng wǒ xiào le.」 Dì èr tiān wǒ yòu diǎn le tóng yì jiā diàn, bèizhù xiězhe「háishi nàge ài chī là de rén」. Zhè cì zhǐtiáo shàng xiězhe:「Yòu shì nǐ! Zhè cì fàng le sì bèi. Bié zài kū le.」 Wǒ juéde, yǒu shíhou ràng yì tiān biàn hǎo de búshì shénme dà shì, érshì yí gè mòshēng rén gēn nǐ kāi de yí gè xiǎo wánxiào.
text_en: I ordered delivery with a note: 'Please add extra spicy.' When it arrived, I found a small note inside: 'Already added triple the spice. If you still think it's not enough, I suggest eating chili peppers directly.' I laughed for a long time. Later I gave a five-star review: 'The spice made me cry, but the note made me laugh.' The next day I ordered from the same place, with the note: 'Still the spice-loving person.' This time the note said: 'It's you again! Quadruple this time. Stop crying.' I think sometimes what makes a day better isn't anything big, but a small joke from a stranger.

title: The Grandma Who Learned Video Calls
text_zh: 我教了奶奶怎么用手机视频通话。第一次她打给我的时候，我只看到了她的额头——因为她把手机举得太高了。我说：「奶奶，往下一点。」她往下了，但是往下太多了，我看到了她的下巴。我们花了五分钟才让她的脸出现在屏幕中间。她很开心地说：「哇，我能看到你了！你瘦了！」我说：「奶奶，那是因为你把手机拿歪了，我被拉长了。」她说：「不对，就是瘦了，多吃点。」后来我发现，不管我怎么教她，每次视频通话她都会说「你瘦了，多吃点」。也许学视频通话不是为了看得更清楚——是为了有一个新的方式说「我想你了」。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiāo le nǎinai zěnme yòng shǒujī shìpín tōnghuà. Dì yī cì tā dǎ gěi wǒ de shíhou, wǒ zhǐ kàn dào le tā de étóu——yīnwèi tā bǎ shǒujī jǔ de tài gāo le. Wǒ shuō:「Nǎinai, wǎng xià yìdiǎn.」 Tā wǎng xià le, dànshì wǎng xià tài duō le, wǒ kàn dào le tā de xiàba. Wǒmen huā le wǔ fēnzhōng cái ràng tā de liǎn chūxiàn zài píngmù zhōngjiān. Tā hěn kāixīn de shuō:「Wā, wǒ néng kàn dào nǐ le! Nǐ shòu le!」 Wǒ shuō:「Nǎinai, nà shì yīnwèi nǐ bǎ shǒujī ná wāi le, wǒ bèi lā cháng le.」 Tā shuō:「Bú duì, jiùshì shòu le, duō chī diǎn.」 Hòulái wǒ fāxiàn, bùguǎn wǒ zěnme jiāo tā, měi cì shìpín tōnghuà tā dōu huì shuō「nǐ shòu le, duō chī diǎn」. Yěxǔ xué shìpín tōnghuà búshì wèile kàn de gèng qīngchǔ——shì wèile yǒu yí gè xīn de fāngshì shuō「wǒ xiǎng nǐ le」.
text_en: I taught grandma how to use video calls on her phone. The first time she called me, I only saw her forehead — because she held the phone too high. I said: 'Grandma, lower it a bit.' She did, but too much — I saw her chin. It took five minutes to get her face in the middle of the screen. She happily said: 'Wow, I can see you! You've gotten thin!' I said: 'Grandma, that's because you're holding the phone crooked — I'm being stretched.' She said: 'No, you're definitely thinner, eat more.' Later I realized that no matter how I teach her, every video call she says 'you've gotten thin, eat more.' Maybe learning video calls isn't about seeing more clearly — it's about having a new way to say 'I miss you.'
